coredata.dc:title: Aptamer based ratiometric determination of DON by exploiting the FRET between carbon dots and graphene oxide
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Meijun
Wang, Tianlin
Li, Tiange
Ma, Yan
Huang, Xianqing
Wan, Libin
Wang, Fayun
Song, Lianjun
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric aptasensor for deoxynivalenol (DON) detection was developed based on FRET between graphene oxide (GO) and carbon dots. Blue carbon dots, functionalized with aptamers, act as energy donors, while GO functions as the energy acceptor. In the absence of DON, the addition of GO quenches the fluorescence of the blue carbon dots, while the fluorescence of red carbon dots, used as internal reference signals, remains unchanged. When DON is introduced, the aptamer selectively binds to the target, increasing the distance between the donor and acceptor, disrupting FRET, and gradually restoring the blue fluorescence. The sensor demonstrated a strong linear relationship within the concentration range of 0.05–200 ng/mL of DON, with a detection limit of 14.7 pg/mL. The superior analytical performance and high sensitivity indicate the potential of the sensor for DON detection in real sample matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Aptasensor
Ratiometric
Deoxynivalenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay procedures for the detection of DON
FRET process between GO and BCDs
The viability of the ratiometric aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of GO. (B) UV–vis spectra of GO. (C) FTIR spectra of BCDs. (D) TEM images of BCDs. (E) Fluorescence spectra of BCDs and BCDs@apt. (F) Agarose gel electrophoresis of BCDs@apt (1) and aptamer (2).
(A) The UV–visible absorbance of GO and emission spectra of BCDs. (B) The fluorescent decay curves of BCDs in the absence and presence of GO.
Fluorescence emission spectra for diverse systems. Inset: Fluorescence images of diverse systems.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the R/BCDs@apt-GO aptasensor to DON concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 200 ng/mL. (B) Fluorescence color changes after adding different doses of DON. (C) The CIE chromaticity diagram depicts the fluorescence spectra of the aptasensor with varying concentrations of DON. (D) Relationship between DON concentration (0.05–200 ng/mL) and F473/F575.
(A) The selectivity of the aptasenor toward DON. The concentration of the interference was 5 times of DON. (B) Stability evaluation of the FRET-based aptasensor within 30 days at 4℃.
Schematic diagram of ratiometric aptasensor based on R/BCDs@apt-GO for DON detection.
The analysis results for DON in grain samples.

head.ce:title: Aptamer based ratiometric determination of DON by exploiting the FRET between carbon dots and graphene oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Engineering Technology Research Center of Food Processing and Circulation Safety Control, College of Food Science and Technology, Henan Agricultural University, Zhengzhou 450002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Engineering Technology Research Center of Food Processing and Circulation Safety Control, College of Food Science and Technology, Henan Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Engineering Technology Research Center of Food Processing and Circulation Safety Control, College of Food Science and Technology, Henan Agricultural University, Zhengzhou, 450002. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Academy of Sciences, Zhengzhou 450002, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Academy of Sciences, Zhengzhou, 450002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric aptasensor for deoxynivalenol (DON) detection was developed based on FRET between graphene oxide (GO) and carbon dots. Blue carbon dots, functionalized with aptamers, act as energy donors, while GO functions as the energy acceptor. In the absence of DON, the addition of GO quenches the fluorescence of the blue carbon dots, while the fluorescence of red carbon dots, used as internal reference signals, remains unchanged. When DON is introduced, the aptamer selectively binds to the target, increasing the distance between the donor and acceptor, disrupting FRET, and gradually restoring the blue fluorescence. The sensor demonstrated a strong linear relationship within the concentration range of 0.05–200 ng/mL of DON, with a detection limit of 14.7 pg/mL. The superior analytical performance and high sensitivity indicate the potential of the sensor for DON detection in real sample matrices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Aptamer based fluorometric determination of DON by exploiting the FRET between carbon dots and graphene oxide

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Deoxynivalenol (DON), a harmful secondary metabolite, is also referred to as vomitoxin due to its ability to cause vomiting. It is mainly produced by Fusarium oxysporum and Fusarium graminearum (Yu et al., 2023a; Zhao et al., 2022). DON poses significant health risks to both humans and animals because of its thermal stability and toxicity, which remain intact even after standard food processing and cooking methods (Hao et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2023). DON is commonly present in cereal products such as corn, wheat, and oats. Prolonged exposure to DON can lead to adverse effects, including anorexia, immunotoxicity, reproductive toxicity, and inhibition of protein synthesis (Shu et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023b). Traditional DON detection methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Shen et al., 2024), liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Kim et al., 2016), and thin layer chromatography (TLC) (Kappenberg and Juraschek, 2021), offer high accuracy and a broad detection range, but they require extensive sample preparation and analysis time (Rocha et al., 2017). Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) provides a faster response, simpler operation, and no complex sample pretreatment (Qin et al., 2021), but it may suffer from false positives, non-specific reactions, and the need for expensive antibodies with strict storage conditions (Wu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). Hence, there is a need for a simple, sensitive, rapid, and precise method for detecting DON.

The fluorescent assay, valued for its speed, sensitivity, simplicity, and ability for on-site detection, has become widely used in biological diagnostics (Peng et al., 2023), environmental monitoring (Yan et al., 2017), and food safety (Li et al., 2022). Aptamers are single-stranded RNA or DNA molecules that are selected from a random oligonucleotide library through the use of the systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) method. Aptamers are single-stranded RNA or DNA molecules selected from a random oligonucleotide library through the systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) process. In fluorescence detection, short, chemically synthesized oligonucleotides are typically chosen to ensure efficient and effective interaction with target molecules (Zhang et al., 2024). Furthermore, aptamers have low molecular weight, simple change of functional groups, and good environmental tolerance (Chen et al., 2024a; 2024b; Guo et al., 2024). These characteristics have made aptamers popular as recognition probes in the development of biosensors for fluorescence-based assays.

Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) is a non-radiative energy transfer process that occurs through dipole-dipole interactions between excited state donors and ground state acceptors (Pikula et al., 2024). The donor absorbs energy from the excitation light and transfers it to a nearby acceptor. While the acceptor absorbs the energy from the donor, it does not necessarily emit energy as fluorescence (Chu et al., 2024). Common donors and acceptors include quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), organic dyes, metal organic frameworks, precious metal nanoparticles (GNs), and graphene oxide (GO).

Semiconductor fluorescent CDs exhibit distinct optical and colloidal properties, including high fluorescence intensity, stability, and prolonged fluorescence lifetime. As a highly effective FRET energy donor, they are widely utilized in labeling, in vitro research, immunoassays, and medical biosensing applications (Wang et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2024). Sun et al. (2024) developed a FRET-based carbon dot sensor for cystatin C detection, achieving a detection limit as low as 0.4 μg/mL. (Sun et al., 2024). Wang et al. coupled carbon dots with antibodies to detect Escherichia coli O157:H7 through FRET, with a detection limit of 7 CFU/mL, based on antigen-antibody specificity (Wang et al., 2024a; 2024b). GO, a novel carbon material with a single-atom-thick two-dimensional (2D) structure, offers several benefits, including high electron mobility, large surface area, and versatile functional properties (Chen et al., 2024a; 2024b). These attributes make GO ideal for various detection and sensing applications, including the identification of organic small molecules, biomolecules, and inorganic ions. GO effectively reduces background interference in detection and exhibits superior fluorescence quenching compared to many other materials, proving itself as an efficient FRET acceptor (Gade et al., 2022). In terms of detection performance, the GO fluorescence biosensor method surpasses other techniques like polymerase chain reaction, electrochemical sensors, MS, and ELISA (He et al., 2022; Li et al., 2024a; 2024b; 2024c).

In the FRET-based system, BCDs were selected as energy donors, with GO chosen as the energy acceptor due to its outstanding performance. RCDs, emitting orange-red fluorescence, were utilized as an internal reference signal. BCDs functionalized with aptamers were obtained through amide reactions, designated as BCDs@apt. GO is known to interact with ssDNA through π-stacking interactions between the nucleobases and the GO surface (Lee et al., 2011). As shown in Scheme 1, the fluorescence of BCDs is quenched by GO due to the aptamer's adsorption onto the GO surface, which enhances FRET (Zhang et al., 2022a; 2022b). The aptasensor, named as R/BCDs@apt-GO, was employed for DON detection. Upon exposure to DON, the aptamer specifically binds to the target, preventing FRET from occurring. The fluorescence of BCDs progressively increases as the concentration of DON rises, showing a linear correlation within a given range. A visible change in fluorescence color from orange-red to blue is evident to the naked eye. The dual-signal response system has a self-calibration function, reducing the occurrence of false positives and false negatives compared to sensors that rely on a single signal, broadening the detection range and lowering the detection limit (LOD) (Chen et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023).

Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), Aflatoxin M1 (AFM1, ≥98 %), Patulin (PAT), Deoxynivalenol (DON, ≥98 %), Zearalenone (ZEN, ≥98 %), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96 %) were supplied by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium nitrate (NaNO3, ≥98.5 %), polyethyleneimine (PEI, ≥99 %), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), citric acid monohydrate (≥99.5 %), o-phenylenediamine, phosphoric acid, graphite, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, ≥96 %), Hydrochloric acid (HCl ≥ 37 %) were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Corn flour and wheatmeal were obtained from a local supermarket in Zhengzhou. The DON aptamer (5′-COOH-GCA TCA CTA CAG TCA TTA CGC ATC GTA GGG GGG ATC GTT AAG GAA GTG CCC GGA GGC GGT ATC GTG TGA AGT GCT GTC CC-3′) was provided by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The surface morphologies were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a Talos G2 200X and an Apreo electron microscope (Thermo, USA). Fluorescence spectra were acquired with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra was measured using a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence lifetimes were recorded by a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer PLSP920 (Edinburgh, Britain). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was measured a Nexus 410 spectrometer (Thermo, USA).

BCDs were obtained from the previous study with some alterations (Pan et al., 2021). 0.6 g of PEI and 0.6 g of citric acid monohydrate were dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water. Then the solution was heated at 180℃ for 2 hours. After drying at 60℃, BCDs were obtained. After that, the BCDs and DON aptamer were conjugated via an amidation process (Cao et al., 2024). In brief, 100 μL of aptamer (6 μM), 50 μL of EDC (60 μM), and NHS (60 μM) were combined in PBS buffer (300 μL, 1 mM, pH=7.5) and stirred at 25℃ for 30 minutes. Then, BCDs (150 μL, 1.46 mg/mL) were added into the solution and continue stirred for 1 hour. The obtained BCDs-aptamer, referred to as BCDs@apt, was stored at 4°C.

RCDs was synthesized using the hydrothermal method described in the previous report (Wu et al., 2023). Initially, 0.2 g of o-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 29 mL of deionized water, and 1 mL of phosphoric acid was added. The solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160°C for 6 hours. The mixture was filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, RCDs were obtained by undergoing the freeze-drying process.

Graphite was first oxidized to produce graphite oxide, which was then ultrasonically separated to produce GO (Mussa et al., 2020). A typical reaction involves stirring 115 mL of H2SO4 with 2 g of graphite in an ice bath for 15 minutes, then gradually adding 2.5 g of NaNO3 and 20 g of KMnO4 after another 15 minutes. The mixture was stirred continuously in an ice bath for another 15 minutes. After 20 minutes, the mixture was heated to 35°C for 2 hours while vigorously stirring. 230 mL of deionized water was then added to the aforementioned combination. After 20 minutes, 20 mL of 30 % H2O2 was added in the mixture. Finally, 10 % HCl was added to the mixture to achieve a neutral pH before drying at 60°C.

10 µL of RCDs (50 µg/mL) were first combined with 10 µL of BCDs@apt (36.5 µg/mL). Then 10 µL of GO (30 mg/mL) was progressively injected into the solution and reacted for 5 minutes at room temperature. The mixture was distributed in PBS buffer solution (220 µL, 1 mM, pH=7.5) to create the ratiometric aptasensor system (R/BCDs@apt-GO).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay procedures for the detection of DON
ce:section.ce:para: 20 µL of DON solution (0–200 ng/mL) was added to a PBS buffer solution (230 µL, 1 mM, pH=7.5) that contained the R/BCDs@apt-GO aptasensor. After incubating for 5 minutes, the fluorescence spectra of aptasensor was collected at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. A correlation was then established between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F473/F575) and the DON concentrations.

The corn flour and wheatmeal samples were first ground into powder. 1.0 g of samples were dissolved in 10 mL of 60 % (v/v) methanol containing DON standard solutions (0, 1, 10, and 100 ng/mL). After mixing evenly, the mixture was centrifuged for 10 minutes. The supernatant was filtered using a 0.22-micrometer membrane. During the measurement process, introduce 20 µL of the processed sample into the sensing system. Measure the detection phase using the method described in Section 2.7.

The sheet-like microstructures, irregular folds, wrinkles, and ultrathin features of GO were clearly displayed, as shown in Fig. 1A. A characteristic absorption peak for GO was observed at 232 nm in Fig. 1B. In the FTIR spectrum of the BCDs, the stretching vibration of N-H was detected at 3392 cm–1, as seen in Fig. 1 C. Additional bands at 1627 cm–1, corresponding to -NH2, were also present. The signal at 1143 cm–1 indicated the presence of C-N bonds (Zhang et al., 2024a; 2024b; 2024c). The successful synthesis of BCDs was verified by the TEM image in Fig. 1D, which revealed a spherical shape with an average particle size of 2.4 nm. Fig. 1E shows that BCDs exhibited blue fluorescence with an emission maximum at 468 nm. After aptamer modification, the surface polarity of the BCDs changed, causing the fluorescence maximum emission wavelength of BCDs@apt to shift from 468 nm to 473 nm (Li et al., 2024). This shift was primarily due to the covalent attachment of cDNA to the surface of BCDs, altering their surface charge and leading to an increase in size. The BCDs@apt conjugates were further validated using agarose gel electrophoresis, as seen in Fig. 1F. Due to the increased mass-to-charge ratio of BCDs@apt, they migrated more slowly than the aptamer, which was consistent with previous findings (Li et al., 2024). These results confirmed the successful functionalization of BCDs with aptamers. The synthesis of RCDs was verified by the TEM image in Fig. S1, which showed a spherical shape with an average particle size of 2.5 nm. The FTIR spectrum of RCDs is displayed in Fig. S2. The broad absorption peak at 3386 cm–1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of N-H and O-H bonds. Absorption peaks at 1652 cm–1 and 1535 cm–1 are associated with CO and CN stretching vibrations, respectively. The peak at 1226 cm–1 is attributed to C-O-C stretching, while the peak at 1105 cm–1 corresponds to the bending vibration of C-H within the benzene ring. The 2844 cm–1 peak is related to C-H stretching vibrations. Characteristic peaks for P = O and PO4 3– were detected at 1325 cm–1 and 487 cm–1, respectively(Zhang et al., 2022). In Fig. S3, RCDs exhibited red fluorescence with an emission peak at 575 nm. In Fig. S4, after preparation of R/BCDs@apt-GO, the R/BCDs@apt could be found that was adsorbed on the surface of GO, due to the π-stacking interaction between the aromatic bases of the aptamer and the hexagonal cells of GO (Lee et al., 2011).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET process between GO and BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of BCDs by GO, both the fluorescence emission spectrum of BCDs and the UV–visible absorption spectrum of GO were obtained. The strong overlap between the emission of BCDs and the UV absorption of GO indicates that the fluorescence quenching may be caused by either the internal filter effect (IFE) or FRET ( Fig. 2 A). To differentiate between these mechanisms, the changes in the donor fluorescence lifetime were examined (Ali et al., 2025; Yin et al., 2024). Specifically, IFE does not influence the fluorescence lifetime of the donor, while FRET reduces the donor’s fluorescence lifetime (Lang et al., 2024). The fluorescence decay curves of BCDs before and after the addition of GO were compared. As shown in Fig. 2B, the addition of GO decreased the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs from 4.58 ns to 3.91 ns. The shortened fluorescence lifetime suggests that the quenching of BCD fluorescence by GO is due to FRET rather than IFE. Thus, the construction of FRET-based ratiometric aptasensors using BCDs-GO as donor-acceptor pairs is feasible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The viability of the ratiometric aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the functionality of the constructed aptasensor, mixed fluorescence was recorded under different conditions, as shown in Fig. 3. Initially, R/BCDs@apt exhibited blue fluorescence (line a), which shifted to red upon the formation of R/BCDs@apt-GO (line b). When exposed to DON, the blue fluorescence was restored due to the prevention of the FRET mechanism by DON (line c). However, the addition of DON in the absence of GO did not significantly alter the fluorescence of R/BCDs@apt (line d).

To optimize the analytical efficiency of the R/BCDs@apt-GO aptasensor for DON detection, both the incubation time and GO concentration were adjusted. As the concentration of GO increased, the F473/F575 ratio gradually decreased (Fig. S5A). When the GO concentration surpassed 30 µg/mL, there was minimal change in the F473/F575 ratio, indicating that the FRET effect was optimal at this concentration. The incubation time between DON and the aptasensor was also examined to ensure consistent experimental results. When the incubation time reached 5 min, the F473/F575 values leveled off (Fig. S5B). The ideal conditions were determined to be a GO concentration of 30 µg/mL and a 5-min incubation time.

With these optimized parameters, the R/BCDs@apt-GO aptasensor was tested for DON detection. In Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity at 473 nm increased with increasing DON concentration, while the fluorescence at 575 nm, acting as an internal reference, remained nearly unchanged. Under UV light, the fluorescence shifted from red to blue, which could be clearly seen (Fig. 4B), and this shift was confirmed by the CIE coordinates (Fig. 4 C). The linear regression analysis for DON detection, shown in Fig. 4D, indicated a strong correlation between the F473/F575 ratio and DON concentration, with the equation y = 0.1069x + 0.7056 (R2 = 0.9952). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the formula 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank and S is the slope of the regression, yielding an LOD of 14.7 pg/mL. Compared to the BCDs@apt-GO aptasensor, which has an LOD of 33.6 pg/mL and a linear range of 0.1–180 ng/mL, the R/BCDs@apt-GO aptasensor demonstrates a lower LOD and a broader linear range (Fig. S6). Additionally, the fluorescence colors were converted to red-green-blue (RGB) values using a smartphone. As seen in Fig. S7, the B/R values increased linearly with DON concentration (0.1–160 ng/mL), with the regression equation y = 0.4019x – 0.2218 (R2 = 0.9851) and an LOD of 35.9 pg/mL. A comparison with previous reports on DON monitoring (Table S1) shows that the ratiometric aptasensor designed here provides a satisfactory linear range and detection limit, with sensitivity meeting the mycotoxin detection standards for typical food samples.

The selectivity and anti-interference performance of the R/BCDs@apt-GO aptasensor were assessed by testing its response to various mycotoxins in food samples, both in the presence and absence of DON. The aptasensor’s selectivity was evaluated in the presence of ZEN, PAT, AFB1, AFM1, and their mixtures, with the concentrations of the other mycotoxins being five times greater than that of the target. As shown in Fig. 5 A, only DON significantly influenced the fluorescence response, while other fungal toxins had minimal effect. The anti-interference capacity of the aptasensor was also tested by introducing a mixture of different substances. Even in the presence of these potential interferents, the sensor still produced a robust response, indicating that the aptasensor demonstrated excellent selectivity. This performance is attributed to the high binding affinity and specificity of the BCDs conjugated with DON aptamers. The storage stability of the aptasensor was examined by regularly measuring the fluorescence intensity ratio. After 30 days of storage, the sensor maintained a strong fluorescence response (Fig. 5B), confirming its excellent stability.

Cereals are highly susceptible to DON contamination during production, harvesting, transportation, and storage (Liang et al., 2024). As shown in Table 1, standard addition experiments were performed on corn and wheat samples spiked with DON concentrations of 0, 1, 10, and 100 ng/mL to evaluate the effectiveness of the method. The recoveries of DON in these samples ranged from 98.12 % to 102.26 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 4.68 %. These findings indicate that the proposed sensor is a reliable tool for detecting DON in different samples.

In summary, a FRET-based ratiometric aptasensor was developed for detecting DON in grains, with blue carbon dots modified with aptamers acting as energy donors and GO as the energy acceptor. This sensor does not require complex preprocessing, offers quick response times, and is easy to construct and operate. The experimental results, which can be visually observed through the color change from red to blue, highlight its low detection limit (14.7 pg/mL) and wide linear range (0.05–200 ng/mL) for DON detection. Compared to other methods for DON detection using FRET or other techniques, this sensor shows enhanced sensitivity and selectivity. Moreover, it has been successfully used for determining DON in wheat samples. This technology has significant potential for widespread application in food safety monitoring.

Wang Fayun: Investigation, Data curation. Song Lianjun: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Huang Xianqing: Visualization, Supervision. Wan Libin: Visualization, Supervision. Li Tiange: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Ma Yan: Visualization, Supervision. Lu Meijun: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wang Tianlin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32101966), Joint Fund of Henan Province Science and Technology R&D Program (No. 225200810049).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2025.107384.
